content.text: A map is a representation or drawing of some area. It can depict a small area such as a village or town, a bigger area like a district or state, or even very large areas like countries or the whole world. In a map, you view the surface as if you are looking at it from the top. An atlas is a book or collection of maps.

content.title: Key Understanding
content.text: Maps reduce large areas into manageable representations on paper while maintaining proportional relationships between places.

There are several kinds of maps: physical maps show natural features like mountains, oceans, and rivers; political maps show details of countries, states, boundaries, and cities; thematic maps display specific information like population, climate, or resources.

A map uses a scale to represent actual distances. The scale shows how a measurement on the map corresponds to the actual distance on the ground. For example, if a scale reads '1 cm = 500 m', it means that 1 centimetre on the map represents 500 metres in reality.

Different maps use different scales depending on the area they represent. A map of a small city might have a scale of 1 cm = 500 m, while a map of India might have a scale of 1 cm = 500 km. By using the scale with a ruler, you can calculate the actual distance between any two places on the map.

content.title: Using the Scale
content.text: To find real distance: Measure the distance on the map in centimetres, then multiply by the scale factor. For example, if two places are 2.5 cm apart on a map with scale 1 cm = 500 km, the actual distance is 2.5 × 500 = 1,250 km.

Direction is crucial in understanding maps. Most maps have arrows pointing to the four cardinal directions: North (N) at the top, South (S) at the bottom, East (E) to the right, and West (W) to the left. These are called cardinal directions or cardinal points.

content.title: Cardinal and Intermediate Directions
items: Cardinal directions: North (N), South (S), East (E), West (W)
Intermediate directions: Northeast (NE), Southeast (SE), Southwest (SW), Northwest (NW)
Most maps display a directional arrow marked with 'N' pointing to the north

content.text: By using cardinal and intermediate directions along with the scale, you can describe the location of any place on a map relative to another place. For example, 'The museum is southeast of the bank and about 2 km away.'

Maps use symbols to represent different features and landmarks. A symbol is a small drawing or marking that stands for an actual object or feature. For example, a small cross might represent a church, a circle might indicate a city, or a line might represent a river.

Different countries may use different sets of symbols. The Survey of India, the government body responsible for mapping India, has fixed a standard set of symbols for use in Indian maps. Symbols are essential because there is not enough space on a map to draw actual pictures of all buildings and features.

content.title: Common Map Symbols
items: Railway lines: broad gauge, metre gauge, railway stations
Roads: metalled (paved), unmetalled (unpaved)
Boundaries: international, state, district
Water features: rivers, wells, tanks, canals, bridges
Religious buildings: temples, churches, mosques
Government buildings: post offices, police stations
Settlements and graveyards
Natural features: trees, grass, forests

content.text: Mapping the Earth is more challenging than drawing a flat map because our planet is not a flat surface — it is nearly spherical in shape. A globe is a three-dimensional model of the Earth and provides a more accurate representation of the Earth's geography than a flat map.

Unlike flat maps, a globe shows the true shapes of continents and oceans, true distances between places, and the correct directions from one place to another. However, globes are less convenient for detailed study and cannot show as much detail as maps.

content.title: Why We Need Both
content.text: Maps are used for detailed navigation and study of specific areas, while globes provide a true representation of the Earth's spherical shape. Most learning involves using both tools together.

Imagine a market with neat rows of shops arranged in a grid pattern. To meet a friend at a specific shop, you would give both a row number and a shop number within that row. This system of using two numbers to identify a location is called a coordinate system.

Similarly, chess uses coordinates to record moves. Letters (a–h) mark the columns and numbers (1–8) mark the rows. Each square on a chessboard can be uniquely identified by its coordinate, like 'd4' or 'e5'. This same principle is applied to maps and globes using latitude and longitude.

content.title: Coordinate Principle
content.text: A coordinate system uses two numbers or measurements to precisely identify the location of any point. On Earth, these are latitude (north-south position) and longitude (east-west position).

On a globe, you can identify the North Pole at the top and the South Pole at the bottom. Halfway between them runs an imaginary circle called the Equator. If you stand on the Equator and travel towards one of the poles, your distance from the Equator increases.

Latitude is a measure of this distance from the Equator. At any point during your journey from the Equator to a pole, you can draw an imaginary line running east to west, parallel to the Equator. Such a line is called a parallel of latitude because it is parallel to the Equator and draws a circle around the Earth.

content.title: Parallel Circles
content.text: As you move away from the Equator towards either pole, the circles marked by parallels of latitude grow smaller. The Equator is the largest circle formed by a parallel of latitude.

Latitudes are expressed in degrees. By convention, the Equator is assigned a latitude of 0° (zero degrees). The latitudes of the two poles are 90° North (written as 90°N) and 90° South (written as 90°S).

The 90° latitude at each pole represents the maximum distance you can travel north or south from the Equator. All places between the Equator and a pole have latitudes between 0° and 90°. For example, a place might have a latitude of 25°N, meaning it is 25 degrees north of the Equator.

content.title: Key Latitude Values
items: Equator: 0°
North Pole: 90°N
South Pole: 90°S
Places north of Equator: marked with °N (1°N to 89°N)
Places south of Equator: marked with °S (1°S to 89°S)

content.text: There is a clear connection between latitude and climate. Around the Equator, where the latitude is low (near 0°), the climate is generally hot and is also called 'torrid'. As you travel away from the Equator towards one of the poles (in other words, as your latitude increases), the climate becomes more moderate, or 'temperate'.

When you approach the North or South Pole, where latitudes are high (close to 90°), the climate grows colder and is called 'frigid'. This variation in climate at different latitudes is due to the angle at which the Sun's rays strike the Earth, which changes with latitude.

content.title: Climate Zones by Latitude
content.text: Hot climate (Torrid): Near the Equator (low latitudes) | Moderate climate (Temperate): Mid-latitudes | Cold climate (Frigid): Near the poles (high latitudes)

Imagine that you travel from the North Pole to the South Pole by the shortest possible line. Observe on a globe that you could pass through various continents (Europe and Africa, Asia, or other routes) — the distance covered would be the same regardless of which route you take. These lines running from one pole to the other are called meridians of longitude.

Unlike parallels of latitude, which are full circles around the Earth, meridians of longitude are half-circles that run from the North Pole to the South Pole. They do not form complete circles; instead, they form a network of semi-circular lines connecting the two poles.

content.title: Meridians
content.text: Meridians of longitude are imaginary lines running from the North Pole to the South Pole. They measure the distance (in degrees) east or west of a reference line called the Prime Meridian.

topics.topic_name: The Prime Meridian as a Reference

content.text: To measure longitude accurately, we need a reference line, similar to how the Equator serves as a reference for latitude. This reference line is called the Prime Meridian. In the year 1884, several nations decided that the meridian passing through Greenwich, an area of London in England, would become the international standard for the Prime Meridian.

The Prime Meridian is marked as 0° longitude. Just as latitude increases from the Equator towards the poles, longitude increases in value from the Prime Meridian. However, unlike latitude which goes from 0° to 90°, longitude goes from 0° to 180°.

content.title: Historical Context
content.text: Before Greenwich was adopted as the international standard, various countries had their own prime meridians. Ancient India used the Ujjayinī meridian (passing through modern-day Ujjain) as a reference for astronomical calculations, as this city was a renowned centre for astronomy.

Longitude is measured eastward and westward from the Prime Meridian. Moving eastward from the Prime Meridian, the longitude increases from 0° to 180°, with the letter 'E' added (like 15°E, 77°E, 140°E). Moving westward, the longitude also increases from 0° to 180°, but with the letter 'W' added (like 75°W, 120°W).

content.title: Longitude Notation and Range
items: Prime Meridian (reference line): 0°
Eastward from Prime Meridian: 0° to 180°E (with 'E' suffix)
Westward from Prime Meridian: 0° to 180°W (with 'W' suffix)
Example: Delhi is at 77°E, New York is at 74°W, Tokyo is at 140°E

content.text: An important observation: The 180°E and 180°W meridians are the same meridian, located on the opposite side of the Earth from the Prime Meridian. This line is marked as 180° (without the E or W distinction) on maps.

When parallels of latitude and meridians of longitude are drawn together on a globe or map, they form a network that looks like a grid. These lines are collectively called grid lines. The grid allows us to locate any place on Earth by using its latitude and longitude coordinates.

The advantage of this system is that every place on Earth has a unique pair of coordinates — one latitude and one longitude. Using these two values, you can precisely identify and locate any location on the planet, whether it is a city, a landmark, or a geographic feature.

content.title: Unique Location System
content.text: With latitude and longitude together, you can determine the exact location of any place on Earth. For example, Delhi is located at approximately 29°N latitude and 77°E longitude.

topics.topic_name: How the Earth's Rotation Affects Time

content.text: The Earth rotates on its axis from west to east, making a complete rotation every 24 hours. This rotation causes the Sun to appear to move across the sky, creating the cycle of day and night. Because of this rotation, different parts of the Earth experience sunrise and sunset at different times.

At any given moment, while it is morning in one part of the world, it might be noon in another, evening in a third, and night in a fourth. This is why the Earth's surface is divided into time zones based on longitude.

content.title: Day-Night Cycle
content.text: The local time at any place depends on the position of the Sun in the sky relative to that place. As the Earth rotates, each place experiences a full day-night cycle over 24 hours.

Local time at any place is determined by the position of the Sun. As you move from one meridian of longitude to another, the local time changes. Since the Earth completes a full rotation (360°) in 24 hours, it rotates 15° every hour.

This means that for every 15° of longitude you travel eastward, the local time is one hour ahead. Conversely, for every 15° you travel westward, the local time is one hour behind. For example, if it is 12 noon at the Prime Meridian (0°), then it is 1 PM at 15°E and 11 AM at 15°W.

content.title: Time-Longitude Relationship
items: Every 15° of longitude = 1 hour difference in time
Eastward travel = time advances (becomes later)
Westward travel = time goes back (becomes earlier)
Example: At Prime Meridian = 12 PM, At 30°E = 2 PM, At 45°W = 9 AM

content.text: Using local time at every longitude would be inconvenient for a country or the world. Instead, most countries adopt a standard time based on a meridian passing through or near them. The standard time for a large region is typically based on one principal meridian within that country.

For example, India uses Indian Standard Time (IST), which is based on the 82.5°E meridian. IST is 5 hours and 30 minutes (5.5 hours) ahead of Greenwich Mean Time (GMT), which is the time at the Prime Meridian.

The world is divided into time zones that broadly follow the 15° longitude divisions. However, these zones do not follow exact meridians; instead, they respect international borders and country boundaries. Some large countries like Russia, Canada, and the USA span multiple time zones.

content.title: Standard Time Concept
content.text: Standard time allows a country or region to function on a single time. India, despite spanning from about 68°E to 97°E longitude, uses IST (5.5 hours ahead of GMT) as its single standard time for the entire country.

The International Date Line is an imaginary line located approximately at 180° longitude, on the opposite side of the Earth from the Prime Meridian. When you cross the International Date Line, the date changes by one day.

If you cross the Date Line travelling eastward, you subtract a day (for example, from Monday to Sunday). If you cross it travelling westward, you add a day (for example, from Sunday to Monday). This adjustment is necessary because of the time zone system and the rotation of the Earth.

content.title: Date Line Location
content.text: The International Date Line is approximately at 180° longitude but deviates from this meridian in several places to avoid dividing countries into two different dates. The line generally follows the 180° meridian over open ocean.

glossary.definition: A representation or drawing of some area of the Earth, showing it as viewed from above. Maps can depict small areas like villages or large areas like countries.
A book or collection of maps, often organized by region or theme.
The ratio on a map that shows how a distance measured on the map corresponds to the actual distance on the ground. Example: 1 cm = 500 km.
The four main directions on a map: North (N), South (S), East (E), and West (W), also called cardinal points.
Directions between the cardinal directions: Northeast (NE), Southeast (SE), Southwest (SW), and Northwest (NW).
A small drawing or marking on a map that represents an actual object, building, or feature (like a church symbol or river line).
A three-dimensional spherical model of the Earth, providing a more accurate representation of the planet's true shape and geography than a flat map.
A pair of numbers or measurements used to precisely identify the location of a point. On Earth, coordinates are latitude and longitude.
The distance of a place north or south of the Equator, measured in degrees. Ranges from 0° at the Equator to 90°N at the North Pole and 90°S at the South Pole.
An imaginary circle running east to west, halfway between the North and South Poles, marking 0° latitude. It divides the Earth into Northern and Southern hemispheres.
An imaginary line running east to west, parallel to the Equator, connecting all places at the same latitude.
The distance of a place east or west of the Prime Meridian, measured in degrees. Ranges from 0° at the Prime Meridian to 180°E and 180°W.
An imaginary half-circle line running from the North Pole to the South Pole, connecting all places at the same longitude.
The meridian passing through Greenwich, London, established in 1884 as the international reference line marking 0° longitude.
The network formed by parallels of latitude and meridians of longitude drawn together on a globe or map.
The time at a specific place based on the position of the Sun in the sky at that location.
The time at the Prime Meridian (0° longitude), used as a reference for calculating time at other locations worldwide.
The standard time used throughout India, which is 5 hours and 30 minutes (5.5 hours) ahead of GMT, based on the 82.5°E meridian.
A region of the Earth where a common standard time is used, typically spanning 15° of longitude and representing a one-hour time difference.
An imaginary line at approximately 180° longitude where the date changes by one day. Crossing it eastward subtracts a day, westward adds a day.
One half of the Earth. The Prime Meridian divides it into Eastern and Western hemispheres; the Equator divides it into Northern and Southern hemispheres.
The region around the Equator where latitudes are low (near 0°) and the climate is hot.
Regions at mid-latitudes where the climate is moderate, neither too hot nor too cold.
The regions near the North and South Poles where latitudes are high (near 90°) and the climate is cold.

summary_points: A map is a representation of an area viewed from above. The three main components of a map are: scale (showing actual distances), direction (cardinal and intermediate directions), and symbols (representing features).
The globe is a more accurate three-dimensional model of Earth than a flat map because it shows the true shape of the planet and the relative positions and sizes of continents and oceans.

Any place on Earth can be precisely located using a coordinate system of latitude and longitude. Latitude measures distance north or south of the Equator (0° to 90°N/S), while longitude measures distance east or west of the Prime Meridian (0° to 180°E/W).
Parallels of latitude run east to west and grow smaller toward the poles; meridians of longitude run north to south from pole to pole. Together, they form a grid system on the globe.

Local time at any place depends on its longitude. Since Earth rotates 15° per hour, every 15° of longitude represents a one-hour time difference. Most countries use standard time based on a principal meridian for convenience.
The International Date Line, located at approximately 180° longitude, marks where the date changes by one day. Crossing it eastward subtracts a day; crossing westward adds a day.
Latitude is connected to climate: hot climate (torrid) near the Equator, moderate climate (temperate) at mid-latitudes, and cold climate (frigid) near the poles.
